---
title: "Introduction to Particle Physics"
subtitle: "A two-lecture mini-course for Baikal School"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
---

This mini-course introduces the particle-physics language needed for neutrino and astroparticle experiments. It is not a derivation of the full Standard Model Lagrangian. The goal is to understand what particles are, how interactions are encoded, what experiments measure, and why neutrinos are both Standard Model particles and evidence for physics beyond its minimal form.

## Lectures

1. [Particles, Fields, and Interactions](slides/01_particles_fields_interactions.html)
2. [The Standard Model and Neutrinos](slides/02_standard_model_neutrinos.html)

## Handouts

- [Questions after Lecture 1](handouts/01_questions_particles_interactions.html)
- [Questions after Lecture 2](handouts/02_questions_standard_model_neutrinos.html)
- [Formula sheet](handouts/formula_sheet.html)

## Notebooks

- [Event rates and cross sections](notebooks/01_event_rates_and_cross_sections.ipynb)
- [Decay law and lifetime](notebooks/02_decay_law_and_lifetime.ipynb)
- [Neutrino oscillation probability](notebooks/03_neutrino_oscillation_probability.ipynb)

The notebooks are pedagogical and use toy models. They are not precision calculations and do not replace a detector simulation.

## Scope

The conceptual line is:

$$
\text{particles and quantum numbers}
\to
\text{interactions and Feynman diagrams}
\to
\text{cross sections and decay rates}
\to
\text{Standard Model structure}
\to
\text{weak interaction}
\to
\text{neutrinos and oscillations}
\to
\text{why minimal SM is incomplete}.
$$
